articles.title: Researchers discover surprising similarity in how humans and great apes giggle — reflecting ties to common ancestors: study
articles.description: “In a way, we are very similar to other great apes because we’ve been laughing in a similar way for 15 million years,” said study author Chiara De Gregorio, a primatologist at the University of Warwick in England.

articles.title: Future generations being failed if more trees not planted, experts say
articles.description: Statistics show the number of trees being planted is increasing, but conservationists say it is not meeting targets.

articles.title: The Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope Arrives in Florida Ahead of Launch
articles.description: NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope arrived June 21st, at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, marking the start of final prelaunch preparations before liftoff later this summer.

articles.title: Black hole's 'point of no escape' studied with the loudest gravitational waves ever heard
articles.description: The loudest crash of gravitational waves ever heard provides an intriguing way of studying event horizons, the boundaries at which nothing can escape the grip of these cosmic titans.

articles.title: Taking the ‘urban pulse’: A new way to diagnose cities

articles.title: Japan's small cities may face higher care burdens under the compact city policy
articles.description: As populations decline and age across the developed world, compact city strategies, which oversee the consolidation of urban facilities and guide residents toward transit-served hubs, have become mainstream policies. Yet most evidence about their benefits comes from cities that are already compact. Far less is known about the social costs that may arise during the transition to compactness in the small and medium-sized cities (SMCs) most exposed to shrinkage.

articles.title: ROS-producing enzymes guide plant cell division and tissue patterning, gene-editing study shows
articles.description: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) produced naturally during cellular metabolism often cause oxidative damage to cells. However, these molecules also play an important role in normal cellular signaling. While ROS are established as essential signaling molecules in various organisms, their precise role in basic plant development and morphogenesis remains unclear.

articles.title: Massive Data Center Cooks Nearby Residents Alive Amidst Deadly Heatwave
articles.description: A "data heat island effect" is the very last thing we need right now. The post Massive Data Center Cooks Nearby Residents Alive Amidst Deadly Heatwave appeared first on Futurism .

articles.title: 'It sounds so impossible': Student studying fungus that makes users hallucinate tiny people may be on the verge of a scientific breakthrough
articles.description: Live Science spoke with Colin Domnauer, a PhD student in ethnobiology whose unraveling of a mushroom mystery could reveal a new hallucinogenic compound.

articles.title: Far-red radiation and elevated CO2 boost biomass accumulation in young leaf lettuce indoors
articles.description: A new study from scientists at Purdue University reports that far-red radiation, in combination with increased carbon dioxide (CO2) concentration, significantly enhances biomass production in young leaf lettuce grown under controlled environments, offering practical guidance for indoor farming operators enthusiastic about including far-red in their sole-source lighting recipes.

articles.title: Rising heat and humidity challenge energy-efficient data center cooling worldwide
articles.description: Reliable operation of data centers has become essential to nearly all sectors of modern society, including health care, education, government services, power grid operation, banking, defense and disaster relief. New research published in Scientific Reports, led by University of Hawai'i (UH) at Mānoa atmospheric scientists reveals that future climate conditions may reduce the availability of one of the cheapest and most energy-efficient approaches to cooling data centers.

articles.title: Europe's deadly heatwave breaks German record and halts public events
articles.description: Germany's highest ever temperature of 41.3C is recorded provisionally in Saarbrücken, over the border from France.

articles.description: A new study, published in the Journal of Raptor Research, reveals that Africa's smallest diurnal bird of prey, the pygmy falcon (Polihierax semitorquatus), operates within one of the smallest breeding home ranges ever recorded for a raptor.

articles.title: Uranus, Neptune May Be Magma Worlds, Not Ice Giants
articles.description: Uranus and Neptune remain two of the most mysterious objects in the solar system, primarily because they’ve only been visited by NASA’s Voyager 2 spacecraft in 1986 and 1989, respectively. Their “ice giant” moniker comes from longstanding hypotheses that their interiors are comprised of an icy mantle beneath their hydrogen/helium atmospheres. While Jupiter and Saturn are also comprised primarily of hydrogen and helium, Uranus and Neptune are hypothesized to have a layered structure comprised of icy elements within their interiors.

articles.description: Researchers used AI and “virtual unwrapping” to reveal never-before-seen texts inside charred Roman scrolls buried by Mount Vesuvius in 79 A.D., opening a new window into the ancient world. NBC News' Claudio Lavanga reports on the exciting discovery.

articles.title: Integration could be key to computational thinking in students
articles.description: Training in computational thinking can improve a student's ability to tackle complex problems, according to research in the International Journal of Technology Enhanced Learning, which examined both students' perceptions of their own skills and their demonstrated performance. The findings come at a time when higher education faces increased pressure to equip students with transferable skills for a changing workplace.

articles.title: Euclid's New Portrait of the Milky Way's Crowded Bulge
articles.description: The ESA's Euclid space telescope took 26 hours to capture this portrait of the Milky Way's central bulge. This isn't part of its primary mission; instead it's kind of like bonus science. It'll be used in the Roman Space Telescope's gravitational microlensing search for exoplanets. Regardless of the science, it's an impressive image.

articles.title: Dublin Freshman Wins 3rd At World's Largest Science Fair
articles.description: Yookta Pandit won third place and $1200 at Regeneron for creating a wearable system that predicts epileptic seizures.
